title: Create A Unit With A Linked Graphic
summary: Create the unit through unit_manager, create the graphic through graphic_manager, then link the unit sprite field explicitly.
steps: Create the graphic asset first or alongside the unit asset.
Create the new unit from a stable base unit or add a new one.
Assign identity fields such as name or file_name.
Link the unit to the created graphic through the correct sprite field.
pitfalls: Do not create the new unit and forget the sprite linkage.
Do not mutate the base template object in place when the workflow expects a copied or created asset.

title: Create A Particle Marker Asset
summary: Build marker-style assets by creating a graphic with particle-effect settings, then attach it to a lightweight marker unit.
steps: Create the particle or graphic asset through graphic_manager.
Set particle effect name, layer, frame count, animation duration, and sequence type.
Create the lightweight marker unit.
Link the marker unit to the new graphic.
pitfalls: Do not treat the graphic as sufficient when the gameplay feature also needs a unit-side anchor object.
Do not mix particle-graphic configuration into unrelated root orchestration code.

title: Link Main Unit, Dead Unit, And Graphics
summary: For multi-part genie features, create the graphics, create the secondary dead unit, link the graphics to the dead unit, then connect the main unit to the dead unit.
steps: Create the graphics used by the dead or transformed state.
Create the main unit.
Create the dead or secondary unit.
Link the dead unit to the graphic ids.
Link the main unit dead-unit field to the secondary unit id.
pitfalls: Do not wire the dead-unit chain before the component ids exist.
Do not spread the chain across unrelated modules without a clear linking helper.

title: Add A Build Task To A Hero Or Unit
summary: After creating or retrieving the hero unit and the build target unit, add a build task to the hero and point that task at the created target unit.
steps: Create or retrieve the hero unit.
Create or retrieve the target unit that the hero should build.
Add a build task to the hero.
Point the task unit id at the created target asset.
Set work range and search behavior fields explicitly.
pitfalls: Do not configure the build task before the target unit exists.
Do not assume default task values are correct for a custom spell-building flow.

title: Use The Shared ID Registry
summary: Import truly global ids and constants from a central config layer instead of repeating raw numbers across asset modules.
steps: Identify ids that are reused by several systems.
Move those ids to config/ids.py or equivalent.
Import the shared constants from feature modules instead of repeating raw values.
pitfalls: Do not move per-feature temporary values into the global registry unless they are genuinely shared.
Do not duplicate global ids in many feature modules.

title: Save The Workspace And Registry Explicitly
summary: Finish the dat workflow with one explicit save pass for the modified .dat and one registry or audit artifact when supported.
steps: Apply all feature modules through the loaded workspace.
Save the modified .dat explicitly.
Save a registry or audit output if the tooling supports it.
Use configured output locations or ask the user when no path is defined.
pitfalls: Do not rely on in-memory edits without a persistent output pass.
Do not encode one developer pc output path as the general guidance.

title: Choose The Manager By Asset Type
summary: Route unit edits to unit_manager, graphics to graphic_manager, sounds to sound_manager, techs to tech_manager, and civ data to civ_manager, then wrap cross-manager logic in one feature apply module.
steps: Identify which asset types the feature touches.
Perform each asset mutation through the owning manager.
Wrap the combined workflow in one high-level feature apply module.
pitfalls: Do not treat the workspace as one undifferentiated object graph.
Do not blur manager ownership in the explanation of a feature workflow.

title: Link Unit Sounds And Graphics As One Bundle
summary: When a new unit needs a coherent presentation package, wire its graphics and sound ids together in one feature module instead of configuring visuals and audio in unrelated places.
steps: Create or copy the graphics needed for the unit's visible states.
Create or retrieve the sound assets used by the unit's key actions.
Create or copy the unit entry that will own those assets.
Assign the unit's sprite fields and the relevant sound references in the same feature workflow.
Keep the final linkage helper separate from the registry and save orchestration layer.
pitfalls: Do not configure graphics and sounds in distant modules with no single linkage owner.
Do not forget to keep shared sound ids in the central registry when they are reused.

title: Create An Upgrade Variant Chain
summary: Build promoted or upgraded units as a linked chain of base unit, upgraded unit, and optional tech or replacement wiring so progression data stays explicit.
steps: Create or copy the upgraded unit from a stable source entry.
Update stats, graphics, and identifiers for the upgraded variant in its own module.
Wire the progression path through the owning upgrade mechanism, such as a tech effect, replacement mapping, or civ-specific unlock.
Keep the progression chain and its balancing data in separate data/config files.
Save and audit the resulting ids after the whole chain is linked.
pitfalls: Do not mutate the base unit in place when the feature expects a distinct upgraded variant.
Do not bury upgrade-chain ids inside the feature logic when they should live in shared data.

title: Wire Train Location And Production Task
summary: When a feature introduces a new production flow, link the produced unit, the production building or actor, and the required task or train-location fields together in one place.
steps: Create or retrieve the produced unit and the producer unit or building.
Add or adjust the production task or train-location mapping on the producer side.
Ensure the produced unit is reachable through the intended production path and any tech gates are updated.
Keep task wiring and unit-stat edits in separate modules so production flow changes stay readable.
Verify the final producer-to-unit relationship before the workspace save pass.
pitfalls: Do not assume the producer can create the new unit without explicit task or train-location wiring.
Do not mix production-path edits with unrelated unit-balance changes in one file.

title: Apply Tech Effect Unlocks
summary: Use the tech and unit layers together so an unlock or upgrade effect is wired explicitly through the owning tech workflow instead of being implied by naming alone.
steps: Create or retrieve the target unit or stat change that the tech should unlock.
Create or edit the owning tech entry through tech_manager.
Add the required tech effects so the unlock or upgrade path is explicit in data.
Wire any civ or production prerequisites after the tech effect itself is stable.
Keep effect ids, affected unit ids, and balancing values in shared data rather than scattered literals.
pitfalls: Do not assume a new tech unlocks content without an explicit effect path.
Do not hide effect targets and amounts in unrelated setup code.

title: Gate Content By Civilization
summary: Apply civ-specific content by separating shared asset creation from civ gating so the unlock rules stay readable and the underlying units remain reusable.
steps: Create the shared unit or tech content first without embedding civ logic into the asset definition.
Apply the civilization-specific availability through civ_manager or the relevant gating mechanism.
Keep civ gating ids, civ lists, and unlock relationships in a separate config layer.
Verify the civ-limited content through the full production or upgrade path rather than only checking the raw unit.
Keep shared content reusable so non-civ variants can still reference it cleanly.
pitfalls: Do not duplicate near-identical units per civ when the real difference is only availability.
Do not mix civ gating and core asset creation in ways that make maintenance harder.

title: Configure Resource Storage And Costs
summary: Handle resource storage and cost changes through dedicated data edits so item, unit, or building economy behavior can be rebalanced without rewriting unrelated workflows.
steps: Identify whether the feature changes carried resources, stored resources, or creation costs.
Apply the storage or cost edits through the owning unit or tech layer instead of burying them inside feature orchestration.
Keep resource ids, storage amounts, and cost values in a shared data file or balance dictionary.
Separate the economy edit from graphics, sounds, and production-path edits so balance passes stay focused.
Verify the resulting resource behavior through the gameplay flow that consumes or grants the resource.
pitfalls: Do not scatter cost and storage values across many modules when they belong in shared balance data.
Do not bundle economy edits with unrelated presentation changes.

title: Create A Tech-Gated Production Chain
summary: Wire a new produced unit through its producer, train task, and gating tech together so the unlock path is explicit from production source to technology requirement.
steps: Create or retrieve the produced unit and the producer building or actor.
Wire the production task or train-location path on the producer side before testing the unlock.
Create or edit the owning tech and add the effect path that exposes the produced unit.
Keep the gating tech ids, producer ids, and unit ids in shared data rather than scattered literals.
Verify the full chain from tech research to production availability rather than validating only one layer.
pitfalls: Do not configure the tech unlock without also wiring the producer-side train path.
Do not split production ownership and tech ownership across unrelated modules with no final linkage helper.

title: Apply A Civilization-Specific Cost Profile
summary: Separate shared unit definitions from civilization-specific economy edits so one civ can pay different costs or use different balance values without duplicating the whole unit asset.
steps: Create the shared unit or building asset first without embedding civ-specific cost logic into the base definition.
Store civ-specific cost values or resource-profile data in a shared balance layer keyed by civilization.
Apply the civilization-specific cost or unlock profile through the owning civ or tech workflow.
Keep the shared unit reusable so only the economy or availability layer differs by civilization.
Verify the resulting civ-specific production flow through real creation or research paths.
pitfalls: Do not duplicate full unit assets when the only difference is economy or availability.
Do not hide civ-specific values inside orchestration code when they belong in shared balance data.

title: Create A Command And Task Replacement Chain
summary: Model custom behavior changes as an explicit command-task chain so the acting unit, target unit, and replacement or custom task path stay readable and maintainable.
steps: Identify the acting unit and the target unit or structure that the command chain should affect.
Create or edit the needed task entries through the owning unit workflow rather than scattering task edits across modules.
Wire the command or replacement path explicitly so the gameplay action reaches the intended target asset.
Keep command ids, task ids, and target ids in shared data or feature-local dictionaries.
Verify the full gameplay path that exercises the command instead of validating only the raw data row.
pitfalls: Do not bury task ids and command ids in one-off setup code when the chain needs later balancing or reuse.
Do not validate only the target unit row when the acting unit or command path is what actually makes the feature work.
